[2023-04-21 16:50] sano712 (sano): Why does Q prefer to soak 3 perfs over 2 splitters? I don't recall seeing this before
[2023-04-21 16:51] sano712 (sano): it's a situation where I wouldn't expect Q defense to screw up so I would maybe not notice this if in time trouble
[2023-04-21 16:54] silentslayers: it doesnt have the intelligence to know that those are permanent attackers instead of soak because u cant rebuy the perfs. not shocking this interaction at all. I would prefer this default Q. If you are assuming it should react based on if you can rebuy perfs i dont know what to tell you 😛
[2023-04-21 16:55] lyra1712 (Lyra): i agree Q shouldnt take into account whether you can rebuy perf or not, but i think id rather it soak splitter over perf always ?
[2023-04-21 16:56] lyra1712 (Lyra): it's clearly not the worst Q defense offender tho
[2023-04-21 16:56] silentslayers: what? no way would i prefer that
[2023-04-21 16:57] lyra1712 (Lyra): i mean, obviously there exists situations where either would be correct
[2023-04-21 16:57] lyra1712 (Lyra): the question is what is more often correct / what is more intuitive
[2023-04-21 16:57] sano712 (sano): I thought that Q usually prioritised keeping maximum damage alive, unless it was stamina based
[2023-04-21 16:57] sano712 (sano): perf can be a permanent attacker if you want it to be
[2023-04-21 16:58] sano712 (sano): it's weird for Q to dismiss it as a perm attacker like that
[2023-04-21 16:58] silentslayers: A splitter is more valuable than a perferator if the red isn't free, no?
[2023-04-21 16:58] silentslayers: seems obvious to keep splitters to me

[2023-04-21 16:58] sano712 (sano): and I thought keeping perfs was it

[2023-04-21 16:59] silentslayers: like say im holding everything cause they have lots of lifespan 1 stuff (plexo etc)

[2023-04-21 17:02] sano712 (sano): but it's a bit of a subjective call
[2023-04-21 17:02] lyra1712 (Lyra): Q hates to soak 0 stam deadeye, for example
[2023-04-21 17:02] lyra1712 (Lyra): because deadeye is expensive
[2023-04-21 17:02] silentslayers: its a priority list probably

[2023-04-28 01:28] weill0 (Weill): Prismata initially seems to be a simple game of numbers, but it's surprisingly deep. It can be very hard to truly understand why you won or lost, even for experienced players
[2023-04-28 01:30] weill0 (Weill): 307th wrote some great articles explaining fundamental strategy concepts. Here's one of them: https://prismatalibrary.blog/2017/12/22/the-standard-style/
The Standard Style
This guide will teach you the standard style of play – a style of play that is simple enough for a beginner to be able to learn, while still being grounded in real high level Prismata strateg…
[2023-04-28 01:32] weill0 (Weill): The short version: you should respect the biggest absorber in the set. You might not always buy it (if the set is aggressive enough), but if you plan to, then you need to plan your economy and tech for it, as well as what else to use the tech for afterwards.
[2023-04-28 01:33] weill0 (Weill): (This is good advice I should take myself! I tend to be too aggressive lol)
[2023-04-28 01:35] weill0 (Weill): Try not to worry too much about winrate. (Also advice I should take myself!) Prismata has a small, dedicated following but few newcomers, you'll tend to be matched against experienced players

threads.formatted_text:
--- Thread (1 messages, 2023-05-21) ---
[2023-05-21 11:22] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): Turn 7 had so many mistakes...

1. You're being attacked. You should defend. Even if you're breached, each wall bought would save you 3 drones, which is a great deal. Pretty sure it's better than Odin, and that's even if Odin was good.

2. Odin was bad. Odin is burst damage, good when opponent has trouble defending. Not when opponent has 21 engineers just sitting there.

3. If you do buy Odin - giving up on 1 rhino and buying 3 forcefields instead is better.

4. Also, you should click Steelsplitter. Losing SS is slightly better than losing 3 drones, but clicking it would kill 1 engineer for antima, so you would actually lose 2 drones, which is better then losing SS.

5. If you don't buy Odin, you can even avoid being breached.

Also: Your thermite cores didn't do anything. You either don't click them and kill engis now, or click them and get pixies which will kill engis later. Doesn't change anything, they didn't actually let you deny absorb. They're a waste of the 1 gold they cost, and that's not even including the B which may or may not have had a better use.

It seems you were thinking that since opponent was overdefended you want long-term units, which is good, but thermite cores are an illusion for this purpose. They give up X attack now for X attack later, when what you want is giving up X attack now for >X attack later. They're only good for denying absorb, exploiting lack of granularity, this sort of thing - which they didn't do here.

[2023-05-27 23:20] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): What I'm trying to say is that, if we define a mistake as something that causes a game to be lost, it is impossible to make a mistake in an already lost position (as you're dead regardless of what you do).
[2023-05-27 23:21] pikachumemes. (Pikachu Memes): it's like critizing buying tia in a desperate lost position--even if it isn't "best" might as well go for broke since you're losing regardless
[2023-05-28 01:40] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): Oh good, we have a philosophy debate on our hands 🙂

The thing is, there are many ways to define “mistake”. You seem to be alluding to a game-theoretic mistake, which lowers the game-theoretic value of the position. It’s a possible definition, but since nobody here plays game-theoretically perfectly, its usefulness is quite limited.

More relevant is what I'll call “player mistake”. Given a player profile - either a bot algorithm, or a set of human players at some Elo level - you can define the player-value of a position as the expectation of the outcome given that both sides play according to the profile. In the case of an algorithm this can be rigorously mathematically defined, and even computed given enough resources. A player-mistake is a move that lowers the player-value of the position.

What constitutes a “mistake” is then dependent on the chosen player profile. It is entirely possible for a move to be a game-theoretic mistake, while being the best move for an actual human to play. The lower the level of the human in question, the more common this is.

You could argue that in the game in question, turn 7 is already 100% lost even if play is continued by players of T-Flash’s level; so it’s not even a player mistake. That’s a fair point, which leads me to the type of mistake which fundamentally is what I had in mind:

I’ll call a “pedagogic mistake” a move which, under conditions similar to those in question, is often a mistake (of one of the other kinds).

For example, you could think of positions where the player is heavily attacked and can choose between buying Odin or buying 3 walls, but where the conditions are more favorable to that player than in the game we examined. In those positions it may be that Odin = Loss and 3 Walls = Win. So if the player learns not to buy Odin in such situations, they’ll improve their win rate, that is, become better players.

You could say that the specific game is not interesting, and my comments were not about the game at all, but rather on the internal “algorithm” that the player was running to find the moves they did. That algorithm lead to buying Odin in this situation, and this is a bug in the algorithm, one which the player is best served by us pointing it out and fixing it.

Of course, this whole “could be a mistake under similar conditions” is fuzzy and ill-defined, but that’s perfectly fine, because we’re humans. My commentary (hopefully) supplied the needed information about how to recognize similar positions and reason about them.

And there are other relevant types of mistakes, which I might write about in a bit.

[2023-06-22 21:34] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): Good news, everyone!

With the cache upgrade behind us, we’re preparing to tackle the harder, long overdue problem of upgrading the game’s database.

The current DB version has been deprecated for 3 years. This was one of the main sources of difficulties in migrating the servers half a year ago, and could surely lead to more problems down the road. In fact it is so old and unsupported that even if we want to upgrade it to an up-to-date version, the usual tools for upgrading it just don’t work.

Luckily, there are other tools to do such an upgrade, and our preliminary testing suggests they should work. This will be a somewhat involved multi-step process, which will take several hours even in the best case scenario - and to prevent loss of data, the game will have to be offline during that period.

If accomplished successfully, this will make future maintenance much easier, and greatly reduce the risk that any future technical changes will take Prismata down permanently.

We currently plan to start the process on Monday, June 26, probably around 2 PM UTC.
